meta.title: What Your Small Business Website Actually Needs in 2026
meta.description: Most business websites don't generate clients. Here is what separates a site that works from one that just exists. By Alex, web developer in Rome.

title: What Your Small Business Website Actually Needs in 2026
lead: Most business websites are online brochures. They look presentable and do almost nothing to bring in clients. Here is what separates a site that works from one that just exists.

sections.heading: The uncomfortable truth about most business websites
paras: A site that exists is not the same as a site that works. The difference is not design — it is intent.
A working site answers questions, builds trust before anyone calls, ranks on Google so the right people find it, and makes the next step obvious. Most sites fail at all four.
The good news: what makes a site work is a set of decisions made during the build — not something you add later.

sections.heading: Seven things your website must have
paras: Speed: loads under 2 seconds on mobile. Fails this, and most visitors leave before reading a word. Core Web Vitals directly affect Google rankings.
Mobile-first layout: designed for a 390px phone screen — not adapted from desktop. 70%+ of your visitors arrive on mobile.
One clear next step per page: book a call, send a message, see the menu. One CTA. Not five.
Trust signals: real client reviews, real photos, a named person behind the business, a physical location.
Contact options that work: clickable phone number, WhatsApp link, email — not just a contact form that disappears into spam.
SEO foundation: title tags, LocalBusiness Schema Markup, verified Google Business Profile, XML sitemap. This is how Google decides your site exists.
Ownership: you own the code and domain. Not a subscription, not a rental. Move, change, or hand it to anyone — no permission needed.

sections.heading: What changed with AI search
paras: In 2026, a growing share of people find businesses through AI: ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews. These systems extract answers from structured, authoritative content — not from pretty pages.
For an AI to recommend your business, your site must be factually clear: who you are, what you do, where, and why you are trustworthy. Vague marketing language is invisible to AI engines.
LocalBusiness Schema Markup is critical here. It tells AI systems your business is real, with a real address and verified service offering. I include it on every site I build.

sections.heading: Bookings and online payments: growing expectations
paras: For service businesses — clinics, salons, restaurants, consultants — online booking is no longer a nice addition. Clients expect it.
A client who finds your site at 11pm should book without calling you. A deposit should be payable without a bank transfer.
These are integrations, not complex builds: a booking calendar connected to your schedule, a payment link connected to your account. Built once, they run themselves.

sections.heading: Owning versus renting your website
paras: A site on Squarespace, Wix, or a similar platform is technically rented. If the platform changes pricing, features, or shuts down, you are affected and cannot move it.
A custom-built site gives you the source code, hosting credentials, and full portability. Move hosts, change developers, hand it to anyone. It belongs to you.
Maintenance cost on modern infrastructure: ~€15/year for the domain. Nothing else.
I hand over everything via GitHub at launch: source code, design files, hosting access. Fully documented, fully yours.

items.q: WordPress or custom? Which is better for a small business?
items.a: WordPress suits content-heavy businesses comfortable managing it themselves. Custom sites are faster, easier to maintain, and don't break from plugin updates. For most small businesses with stable content, custom is the better long-term investment.
Not necessarily. A blog helps only if you publish content your clients are actively searching for. Three well-written evergreen guides outperform a neglected blog with 50 generic posts.

items.q: How often should I update my website?
items.a: Keep your core pages (services, contact, about) current at all times. Beyond that, add content when you have something genuinely useful to say — not on a forced schedule.

items.q: Do I need a website if I already have Instagram?
items.a: Yes. Social platforms change algorithms, throttle organic reach, and can be blocked. Your website is the only online presence you fully control. Instagram drives traffic to it but cannot replace it.

items.q: What is a CTA and why does it matter?
items.a: CTA = call to action: the specific thing you want a visitor to do next. One clear CTA per page dramatically increases the share of visitors who actually contact you.

items.q: How do I know if my current website is actually working?
items.a: Connect it to Google Search Console and Google Analytics — both free. Search Console shows which searches bring visitors. Analytics shows what they do when they arrive. Bounce rate above 80% means something is broken.

items.q: Can I build my own website instead of hiring someone?
items.a: You can — Squarespace and Webflow make it possible without technical skills. The trade-off: you'll hit limits faster than expected, and Core Web Vitals and SEO performance rarely match a custom build.

items.q: What information do I need to prepare before working with a developer?
items.a: Services and pricing range, a short description of what makes you different, the city or area you serve, any existing photos or logo, and examples of sites you like. That is enough to start.

authorBio: Written by Alex, web developer based in Rome, Italy. I build websites that actually bring in clients, not just sit online.
